segments.purgatorio-8-g60: Interjection expressing surprise or exclamation; kept in quotation marks as direct speech. It has no grammatical connection beyond marking an emotive utterance.
segments.purgatorio-8-g61: A single comma functioning as the clause separator and as the opening punctuation for the reported speech that follows.
segments.purgatorio-8-g62: diss' is a poetic contraction of the passato remoto 'dissi' (I said); the verb is placed before the subject 'io' (inversion common in poetry). 'lui' is the tonic pronoun used as the indirect object ('to him') — the preposition 'a' is omitted, which is a feature of more elevated/archaic style.
segments.purgatorio-8-g63: 'Per entro' is a compound, somewhat archaic/prepositional phrase meaning 'through/into' these places; it governs the following noun phrase. 'venni' is passato remoto, first person singular, 'I came'; 'stamane' is an adverb modifying 'venni' ('this morning'). The order places the adverb after the verb, which is normal in Italian poetry.
segments.purgatorio-8-g64: 'E' links the clauses ('and'). 'Sono' is present indicative of 'essere' (I am). 'In prima vita' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in my first life' (prima = 'first' placed before vita). The phrase states Dante's condition as presently living in his original life.
segments.purgatorio-8-g65: 'Ancor che' here functions as a concessive conjunction meaning 'although'/'even though'. 'L’ altra' is elliptical for 'the other (life)' — the noun 'vita' from the previous clause is understood. The article is elided in orthography as 'l’'.
segments.purgatorio-8-g66: 'Sì' is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' (here 'as/while'), and 'andando' is the gerund of 'andare' ('going/proceeding'). Together they form an adverbial phrase 'sì andando' = 'in going on/as I proceed', modifying the verb in the subordinate clause.
segments.purgatorio-8-g67: Acquisti is the present subjunctive form (3rd person singular) of 'acquistare' used because the concessive 'ancor che' governs the subjunctive; it refers back to 'l'altra' ('the other [life]') and means 'may acquire' or 'shall gain' in sense (will come to possess) — here 'gain' in a figurative sense.
segments.purgatorio-8-g68: 'E come' = 'and when/as'. 'Fu ... udita' is a passive/perfect construction in passato remoto: 'was heard'. 'La mia risposta' is the feminine noun phrase that agrees with the past participle 'udita' (feminine singular). Word order places the temporal clause before the main clause.
segments.purgatorio-8-g69: Two people are named as the subjects ('Sordello ed elli' = 'Sordello and he'). 'In dietro' is an adverbial phrase 'backward/behind'. 'Si raccolse' is reflexive (si) with 'raccolse' in passato remoto, literally 'gathered themselves' but here 'drew back/withdrew'. The simile 'come gente di sùbito smarrita' uses 'gente' (a collective singular) and 'smarrita' is a past participle/adjective agreeing in the feminine singular with 'gente' meaning 'suddenly lost/bewildered.'
segments.purgatorio-8-g70: L'uno = 'the one' / 'one' and 'l'altro' = 'the other'. 'A Virgilio' and 'a un' indicate the persons they turn to ('to Virgil' and 'to a certain man'). 'Si volse' is the reflexive passato remoto of 'volgersi' ('turned [himself]') and 'che sedea lì' is a relative clause: 'who was sitting there'; 'sedea' is the imperfect tense ('was seated/sitting'), giving background continuous action.
segments.purgatorio-8-g71: Gridando is a present participle/gerund ('shouting'), used adverbially to indicate how they addressed the man — it modifies the action of turning and introduces the quoted cry.
segments.purgatorio-8-g72: Sù is an interjection here meaning 'Up!' or 'Come on!' used to urge the addressee to rise or attend; it functions as part of the direct speech that follows.
segments.purgatorio-8-g73: A vocative: the proper name 'Currado' is being directly addressed by the shouters. It stands alone as the addressee of the imperative/interjection.
segments.purgatorio-8-g32: Ambo = 'both' (subject). vegnon is an archaic/poetic 3rd-person plural present tense (vegnon < venire) meaning 'they come' (modern Italian: vengono). del grembo di Maria is a prepositional phrase: del = di + il ('from/of the'), grembo = 'womb/bosom', di Maria = 'of Mary' (possessive). The whole segment names the origin of the two people.
segments.purgatorio-8-g33: disse is the simple past (passato remoto) 3rd-person singular of dire: 'said'. It introduces reported speech. Sordello is the speaker's name in apposition.
segments.purgatorio-8-g34: a guardia de la valle is a prepositional phrase giving purpose/role: a = 'to/for', guardia = 'guard/watch', de (di) = 'of', la valle = 'the valley'. Grammatically it marks the role those two perform (they come as guardians of the valley).
segments.purgatorio-8-g35: per lo serpente introduces the reason or threat: per = 'for/because of/against' here meaning 'against' or 'on account of', lo serpente = 'the serpent'. che verrà vie via is a relative clause modifying serpente: che = 'that', verrà = future 3rd sing. of venire 'will come', vie via is a poetic adverbial repetition meaning progressively/increasingly (literally 'way by way'), so 'that will come more and more.'
segments.purgatorio-8-g36: Ond' (ond = ondi/ond(e) contracted) here functions as 'thereupon'/'so'/'therefore' — a discourse connector. io is the subject pronoun 'I', emphatic after the connector.
segments.purgatorio-8-g37: che non sapeva per qual calle is a relative clause modifying io: che = 'who/that', non sapeva = imperfect 'did not know' (backgrounded knowledge state), per qual calle = 'by/which path' (per = 'by/through', qual = 'which', calle = 'path/lane').
segments.purgatorio-8-g38: mi volsi intorno is a reflexive perfect/simple past: volsi (passato remoto of volgere) = 'I turned', mi = reflexive clitic 'myself', intorno = 'around'. Together: 'I turned about.'
segments.purgatorio-8-g39: e stretto m’accostai joins two coordinated actions: e = 'and'; stretto = an adverb/participial adjective here 'close' modifying the manner; m' is a contracted mi (reflexive/clitic), accostai = passato remoto 1st sing. of accostare 'I drew near / I approached'. The order stretto m'accostai is idiomatic: 'I pressed myself close.'
segments.purgatorio-8-g40: tutto gelato is a predicative phrase: tutto = 'completely/entirely' modifying gelato = past participle 'frozen' (here meaning numb or chilled). It describes the speaker's physical state.
segments.purgatorio-8-g41: a le fidate spalle is a prepositional phrase marking the target/location of the action: a = 'to/against', le = definite article 'the', fidate = adjective 'trusted' (agreeing in gender/number), spalle = 'shoulders'. Literally 'to the trusted shoulders.'
segments.purgatorio-8-g42: E = 'and'; Sordello is the proper name (subject of the following speech); anco = archaic form of ancora = 'also/too' (adds that Sordello also speaks). The colon marks direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-8-g43: Or avvalliamo omai tra le grandi ombre begins the reported command/suggestion. Or = 'now'; avvalliamo is an irregular/archaic verb here used for motion or descent (Dante's usage), 1st-person plural imperative/suggestive 'let us ...' (the lemma list given doesn't fit exactly); omai = 'by now/now'; tra = 'among'; le grandi ombre = 'the great shades' (ombra = shade/spirit). Grammatically it's an exhortation to move among the shades.
segments.purgatorio-8-g44: e parleremo ad esse; coordinates the action to follow: e = 'and'; parleremo = future 1st-person plural of parlare 'we will speak'; ad = 'to'; esse (feminine plural demonstrative/pronoun) = 'them' (the shades). It announces they will address those shades.
segments.purgatorio-8-g45: grazïoso fia lor vedervi assai is a clause stating the shades' likely reaction: grazïoso = 'gracious/pleasing/kind' used impersonally; fia = future of essere 'will be'; lor = 'to them/their' (dative/possessive for plural); vedervi = vedere + clitic vi 'to see you' (infinitive + clitic appended); assai = 'very/much'. Together: 'it will be very pleasing to them to see you.'
segments.purgatorio-8-g46: Solo tre passi credo ch’i’ scendesse is a main clause with an embedded subordinate introduced by ch' (che): Solo tre passi = 'only three steps' (quantity phrase), credo = 'I believe/I think' (present), ch'i' scendesse = 'that I descended' where scendesse is the imperfect subjunctive (credo + che often takes subjunctive in older/poetic usage). The clause expresses a recollection or estimate.
segments.purgatorio-8-g47: e fui di sotto is coordinate: e = 'and'; fui = passato remoto of essere 'I was' (here 'I found myself'); di sotto = a locative/positional adverbial 'below'/'beneath'. Together: 'and I was below.'
segments.purgatorio-8-g48: e vidi un che mirava pur me continues the narrative: e = 'and'; vidi = passato remoto 'I saw'; un = 'one/a person'; che mirava = relative clause 'who was gazing' (mirava = imperfect 'was looking/gazing'); pur me = pur (poetic pure) = 'even/still/straight' + me = 'me' (object). It says someone was staring at the speaker.
segments.purgatorio-8-g49: come conoscer mi volesse is a subordinate clause expressing manner or purpose: come = 'as/like/as if'; conoscer = infinitive 'to recognize' (here used in the sense 'recognize'); mi = clitic 'me'; volesse = imperfect subjunctive of volere 'would want' (or 'seemed to want'), creating an indirect expression 'as if he wanted to recognize me.'
segments.purgatorio-8-g19: Io vidi is the simple past (passato remoto) 'I saw' with the subject 'Io' stated for emphasis. Quello essercito gentile is a noun phrase 'that gentle (or noble) host/army' — 'quello'='that' preceding the noun; 'essercito' is an archaic/poetic form for 'esercito' (army) and 'gentile' is an attributive adjective meaning 'noble/courteous'. Tacito poscia riguardare in sùe places the adverb 'tacito' ('silent') and 'poscia' ('afterward') before the infinitive-like verb construction 'riguardare in sùe' meaning 'to look back at them' (riguardare here = 'look back/turn one's gaze'); 'in sùe' = 'upon them' (feminine plural).
segments.purgatorio-8-g20: Quasi is used here as 'as if' (introducing a comparison), and aspettando is the gerund of aspettare, 'waiting' or 'expecting' — together 'as if awaiting'.
segments.purgatorio-8-g21: Palido and umìle are predicate adjectives (plural implicitly agreeing with the host): 'pale and humble/meek.' The conjunction e simply links the two adjectives.
segments.purgatorio-8-g22: E vidi is again 'and I saw' (passato remoto vidi). Uscir and scender are infinitives used as infinitival complements of vidi ('I saw [them] issue/come forth and descend'); de l’alto = 'from on high' (de = from, l’alto = the high place), giùe = 'down'; con due angeli indicates the beings performing those actions, con due spade affocate qualifies what they bore: 'with two spade(s) affocate' — affocate here describes the swords as heated/overheated (poetic sense).
segments.purgatorio-8-g23: Tronche e private de le punte sue are two adjectival/participial modifiers describing the swords: tronche = 'cut off, truncated'; private (past participle of privare) = 'deprived' (here 'stripped of'); de le punte sue = 'of their points' (literally 'of the tips that belong to them'). Word order places the modifiers after 'spade' for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-8-g24: Verdi is the plural adjective 'green' placed before the simile introduced by come ('like'). Fogliette is a diminutive 'little leaves'; pur mo nate = 'just now born' (pur = still/just, mo = now, nate = past participle 'born'), and erano is the imperfect 'they were' — so: 'They were green like little leaves just born, in (their) garment.' Veste = 'garment/robe' (here a covering or 'garb').
segments.purgatorio-8-g25: Che introduces a relative clause modifying 'veste' (or the whole image): 'which, struck by green feathers, trailed behind and were fanned.' Da indicates the agent/instrument 'by' (i.e. struck by), verdi penne = 'green feathers' (penne can mean feathers or wings), percosse is a past participle 'struck', traean is the imperfect 'they drew/they trailed (along)', dietro = 'behind', and ventilate is a participle/adjective 'fanned' or 'being fanned.' The clause describes the movement/animation caused by the feathers.
segments.purgatorio-8-g26: L’un = 'the one' (one of them); poco 'a little' modifies the motion; sovra noi = 'over/above us'; a star is the infinitive construction 'to stand' (a + infinitive indicates purpose or result); si venne is venire in passato remoto with a reflexive particle si used to indicate the action was performed toward a state: 'came to stand/hover.' Word order (verb at the end) is characteristic of poetic diction.
segments.purgatorio-8-g27: E l’altro 'and the other' (article + adjective as pronoun). Scese is the passato remoto of scendere 'descended'. In l’opposita sponda is a prepositional phrase 'to the opposite bank/shore' (in + article + adjective + noun). The phrase describes the complementary motion of the second angel.
segments.purgatorio-8-g28: Sì che is a conjunction meaning 'so that' or 'therefore such that'; la gente 'the people' is the subject of the result clause; in mezzo 'in the middle'; si contenne is the passato remoto of contenere with reflexive si, meaning 'kept themselves' or 'were held' — i.e. the people remained suspended/contained in the middle between the two angels.
segments.purgatorio-8-g29: Ben is an intensifying adverb 'well' or 'clearly.' Discernëa is an archaic/poetic imperfect form of discernere 'I could distinguish/was able to discern' (the diaeresis marks the original meter/pronunciation). In lor = 'among them/in them' and la testa bionda is the direct object 'the fair/blond head' — word order (verb before object) is normal in Dante for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-8-g30: Ma = 'but.' Ne here functions as a locative/pronominal adverb 'in it' or 'therein' referring back to the face/head just mentioned; la faccia = 'the face.' L’occhio is the subject 'the eye' (article + noun), and si smarria is an older/poetic form meaning 'becomes lost' or 'is bewildered' with the reflexive si; grammatically it is a past-in-time form used to describe the narrator's gaze becoming confused. The whole line: 'but in the face my eye was lost/bewildered.'
segments.purgatorio-8-g31: Come introduces a simile 'like/as.' Virtù here means 'power' or 'faculty' (not primarily moral virtue) — the mental faculty of sight or perception. Ch’ = che = 'that' (relative), a troppo = 'by too much' or 'through excess,' and si confonda is the present subjunctive/reflexive of confondere used impersonally/passively: 'is confounded' or 'becomes confused.' The simile compares the narrator's bewildered gaze to a faculty overwhelmed by excess.
segments.purgatorio-8-g50: Temp' is an archaic contraction of 'tempo' (time) and here begins a temporal clause; 'era' is the imperfect ('it was') and with 'già' ('already') sets the scene. 'che' introduces the subordinate clause; 'laere' (here 'aere') is a poetic word for the sky/upper air, with the clitic 's'' (si) marking the reflexive/pronominal construction and 'annerava' the imperfect 'was blackening/greying' describing an ongoing change of light.
segments.purgatorio-8-g51: The phrase 'ma non s�� che' means 'but not so that' (a negative result clause); 'tra li occhi suoi e ' miei' is literal 'between his eyes and mine' (an idiom for exchange of looks), where 'li' is the archaic article and the apostrophe before 'miei' is metrical elision. 'non dichiarisse' is the imperfect subjunctive used in a result clause ('would not declare/reveal'); 'ci��' ('that which') + relative 'che' refers to something previously hidden, and 'pria serrava' ('had earlier kept closed') uses imperfect 'serrava' of 'serrare' meaning 'hold/keep closed, enclose'.
segments.purgatorio-8-g52: 'Ver' here is an archaic clipped form of 'verso' (toward) used adverbially; the order 'Ver' me si fece' literally 'toward me himself he made' uses the reflexive 'si' with 'fece' (passato remoto 3rd sing. of 'fare') to indicate a quick movement—'he turned to face me'—with the clitic 'me' preceding the verb for emphasis/poetic word order.
segments.purgatorio-8-g53: Parallel to the previous clause, 'e io ver' lui mi fei' is 'and I made myself toward him' (I turned to him). 'io' is the explicit subject; 'ver'' again = 'verso' (toward); the reflexive clitic 'mi' with 'fei' (passato remoto 1st sing. of 'fare') forms 'I made myself' = 'I turned'.
segments.purgatorio-8-g54: This is a vocative address: 'giudice' ('judge') is the noun of address and 'gentil' ('gentle' or 'noble') is a postpositive adjective. 'Nin' here is a proper-name/addressing particle (traditionally read as a short form of the name 'Nino' in this passage), so the phrase addresses the man as 'Judge Nino, gentle one.'
segments.purgatorio-8-g55: 'Quanto mi piacque' is an exclamatory perfective statement ('how much it pleased me'); 'piacque' is passato remoto of 'piacere' with indirect object 'mi'. The subordinate temporal clause 'quando ti vidi' ('when I saw you') uses passato remoto 'vidi'; the content seen is expressed by the older construction 'non esser tra 'rei' ('not to be among the guilty'), where 'esser' is the infinitive governed by the perception verb 'vidi' and 'tra i rei' means 'among the guilty ones.'
segments.purgatorio-8-g56: The phrase 'Nullo bel salutar tra noi si tacque' literally means 'no fair greeting among us remained unsaid': 'Nullo' (no) modifies 'bel salutar' (beautiful/pleasant greeting), 'tra noi' ('between us'), and 'si tacque' is reflexive/passive-like 'fell silent' (passato remoto 3rd sing.). The structure places the negated noun phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-8-g57: 'Poi' ('then') sequences the action and 'dimandò' (passato remoto) is the verb 'asked'—Dante often uses 'dimandare' for 'to ask' in narrative past.
segments.purgatorio-8-g58: This direct question uses the periphrastic 'Quant' è che tu venisti' ('How long is it that you came')—a fixed older-Italian formula asking how long ago something happened; 'venisti' is passato remoto of 'venire' ('you came'), 'a piè del monte' ('to the foot of the mountain'), and 'per le lontane acque' ('by/through the distant waters'), where 'le' agrees with the feminine plural 'acque.'
segments.purgatorio-8-g59: This segment contains only a full stop (punctuation) marking the end of the previous sentence and a pause in the poem; there are no lexical items to analyse.
segments.purgatorio-8-g1: Era (imperfect or past tense ‘was’) sets the scene in the past: ‘It was already the hour…’. The clause 'che volge il disio ai navicanti' is a relative clause introduced by che with volge in the present meaning a habitual or characteristic action (‘that turns longing toward sailors’). The coordinate clause 'e ’ntenerisce il core lo dì c’han detto ai dolci amici addio' uses ’ntenerisce (an archaic/informal form of in- + tenerisce) meaning ‘softens’ and lo dì c’han detto… is a temporal relative (literally ‘the day on which they have said goodbye to their sweet friends’).
segments.purgatorio-8-g2: The coordinating 'e che' continues the description: 'and that…'. 'lo novo peregrin d’amore' is the noun phrase subject (the new pilgrim of love), with the article lo and d’ = di. 'punge' is present indicative, third person singular of pungere, here meaning an internal sting or urge (Cupid-like effect) — 'pricks/stings him'.
segments.purgatorio-8-g3: This is a conditional subordinate: 'se ode squilla di lontano…' — 'if he hears a bell ringing from afar'. 'ode' is present 'hears'; 'squilla' (verb) = 'rings out'; 'di lontano' = 'from far away'. The following 'che paia il giorno pianger che si more' is a dependent clause introduced by che: 'that makes the day seem to weep as it dies.' 'paia' is a present subjunctive/indicative of parere used to express appearance ('seems'), and 'pianger' is an infinitive used here as the action (poetic compression) — 'the day seem to weep that dies.'
segments.purgatorio-8-g4: ‘Quand’ io incominciai…’ = 'When I began…' ('incominciai' = I began, past). The verbs that follow are infinitives introduced by a (a render vano, a mirare): 'to make hearing vain' (render vano l'udire = to render listening useless) and 'to gaze' (mirare). 'una de l’alme surta' is the object: 'one of the souls risen up' — 'una' feminine, 'de l'alme' = of the souls, 'surta' (past participle/adjective) = risen/started up.
segments.purgatorio-8-g5: Relative clause modifying 'una de l’alme': 'che l’ascoltar chiedea con mano.' 'l’ascoltar' (the act of listening) is the object; 'chiedea' is the imperfect (archaic spelling) 'was asking/pleading' — it indicates an ongoing request; 'con mano' means 'with (her) hand' i.e. she gestured with her hand to be heard.
segments.purgatorio-8-g6: Simple past narrative: 'Ella giunse' (she arrived/came) and the coordinated past 'levò' (raised, lifted). 'ambo le palme' = 'both palms' (ambo = both; le palme = palms/hands). The verbs are third person past forms describing the single action.
segments.purgatorio-8-g7: 'Ficcando' is the gerund of ficcare and here means 'fixing' (one action concurrent with the main verbs): 'fixing her eyes toward the East.' 'li occhi' is the article + noun 'the eyes'; 'verso l’orïente' = toward the Orient/dawn — the gerund expresses manner.
segments.purgatorio-8-g8: 'come' introduces a comparison or manner 'as if', and 'dicesse' is the imperfect-subjunctive/conditional sense of dire used to report what she seems to be saying: 'as if she were saying'. 'a Dio' marks the addressee 'to God.'
segments.purgatorio-8-g9: Short quoted phrase. 'D’altro' = 'of/from anything else' (elliptical for 'by anything else' or 'with anything else'); 'non' negates; 'calme' is present subjunctive/imperative-form of 'calmare' meaning 'calm' (here used as a prayer/request: 'do not calm me with anything else' or 'comfort me not by other means').
segments.purgatorio-8-g10: The Latin hymn title 'Te lucis ante' is the direct object that 'uscìo di bocca' ('issued from her mouth'). 'Sì devotamente' modifies the manner ('so devoutly'). 'le' functions as a dative/ethical pronoun referring to her (a medieval/dialectal clitic construction: 'to her there issued from the mouth'). 'con sì dolci note' = 'with such sweet tones'.
segments.purgatorio-8-g11: 'che fece me a me uscir di mente;' is a result clause: 'which made me, from myself, go out of my mind' — an idiom meaning 'made me lose my wits.' 'fece' (past of fare) = 'caused/made'; the doubled 'me a me' intensifies the reflexive effect ('me, out of myself').
segments.purgatorio-8-g12: Coordinate continuation: 'e l’altre poi' = 'and the others then'. 'dolcemente e devote' describe their manner (gently and devoutly). 'seguitar lei' (seguitar = archaic/poetic form of seguire) = 'followed her'; 'per tutto l’inno intero' = 'throughout the whole hymn' (per tutto = throughout; intero = entire).
segments.purgatorio-8-g13: 'avendo li occhi a le superne rote' is a circumstantial gerund phrase: 'having their eyes on the supernal wheels.' 'avendo' (having) ties the posture (their gaze) to the action; 'le superne rote' = 'the heavenly/celestial wheels' (a cosmological image).
segments.purgatorio-8-g14: 'Aguzza' is an imperative/form of aguzzare meaning 'sharpen' or 'make keen' — an address to the reader. 'qui' = 'here' (marks the immediate point in the narrative where special attention is required).
segments.purgatorio-8-g15: Vocative address 'lettor' (reader) — an archaic/truncated form of lettore used to call the reader’s attention directly.
segments.purgatorio-8-g16: 'ben li occhi al vero' completes the command: 'well [sharpen] the eyes to the truth.' 'ben' = well; 'li occhi' = the eyes; 'al vero' = toward the truth/reality — a figurative injunction to see things rightly.
segments.purgatorio-8-g17: 'ché' = 'for/since'; '’l velo è ora ben tanto sottile' = 'the veil is now so very thin.' 'è' present of essere; 'ora' = now; 'ben tanto' = emphatic 'so very'; 'sottile' = thin/fine. The clause gives the reason to attend closely.
segments.purgatorio-8-g18: 'certo che ’l trapassar dentro è leggero.' = 'certainly that the passing through inside is light/easy.' 'certo che' introduces certainty; 'trapassar' (infinitive used as noun) = 'to pass through'; 'dentro' = inward/inside; 'è leggero' = 'is light/easy' — i.e. the passage (metaphorical or literal) is easy because the veil is thin.
segments.purgatorio-8-g87: 'Per lei' here means 'with regard to her' or 'about her'; 'assai di lieve' is an archaic adverbial phrase meaning 'very easily' (literally 'much with lightness'); 'si comprende' is the impersonal/passive reflexive construction (si + 3rd-singular) meaning 'it is understood' or 'one understands'; 'quanto ... dura' introduces a subordinate clause 'how long ... lasts' with 'dura' functioning as the verb of duration; 'in femmina' = 'in a woman' and 'foco d'amor' = 'the flame/ardor of love' (genitive 'di' linking amor to foco).
segments.purgatorio-8-g88: This conditional clause begins with 'se' = 'if'; 'l’ occhio' (elided article before a vowel) = 'the eye' and 'o ’l tatto' = 'or the touch'; 'spesso' is an adverb ('often') modifying the verb; 'non l'accende' is negation + clitic + verb: 'non' = not, 'l",
segments.purgatorio-8-g89: This is a negated future: Non + clitic + farà = 'will not make/give.' 'Non' negates the verb farò/farà (future of fare). 'Le' is a dative clitic here ('to her' / 'for her') placed before the verb. 'Sì bella sepultura' is an exclamatory/degree phrase (sì + adjective + noun) meaning 'so/so very beautiful a burial.' The noun phrase 'la vipera' (definite article + noun) is the subject; 'che' introduces a relative clause modifying 'vipera.'
segments.purgatorio-8-g90: Com' is the apocopated form of 'come' ('as'/'like'). Avria is the medieval orthography of the conditional auxiliary (avrebbe) and with the past participle fatto yields a conditional past sense: 'would have done' or more idiomatically 'would have given/done.' 'Il gallo di Gallura' is the subject of that would‑have action. The clause is a comparison: 'as the rooster of Gallura would have done.'
segments.purgatorio-8-g91: 'Così' is an adverb meaning 'thus' or 'so.' 'Dicea' is the imperfect of dire ('he/was saying' or 'he said' in a narrative past), a common narrative tense in Dante that expresses ongoing or habitual past action.
segments.purgatorio-8-g92: 'Segnato' is a past participle used adjectivally/passively ('marked' or 'stamped'). 'De' is the archaic form of 'di' meaning 'of' or 'by' and introduces the agent/source. 'La stampa' is the noun phrase linked to 'segnato' ('marked by the stampa'); here 'stampa' functions as the thing that leaves an imprint (an archaic/poetic usage).
segments.purgatorio-8-g93: 'Nel' is the contraction of 'in + il' ('in the'); 'suo' is the possessive adjective 'his' (agreeing with 'aspetto'); 'aspetto' here means 'appearance' or 'expression.' The whole prepositional phrase specifies where he is 'marked' — in his look/expression.
segments.purgatorio-8-g94: 'Di quel' = 'of that' (genitive), introducing the quality that marks his expression. 'Dritto' modifies 'zelo' with the sense 'right/upright' (an ethical/true zeal). 'Che' is a relative pronoun introducing the clause that describes the zeal. 'Misuratamente' is an adverb 'moderately' and modifies the verb avvampa. 'In core' = 'in (the) heart' (core is an archaic form of cuore). 'Avvampa' is a present indicative (intransitive) meaning 'blazes/glows,' so the phrase means that this upright zeal 'blazes in the heart' in a measured way.
segments.purgatorio-8-g95: 'Li' is an archaic masculine plural definite article ('the'); word order 'Li occhi miei' places emphasis on the possessor after the noun ('my eyes'). 'Ghiotti' is an adjective agreeing with 'occhi' meaning 'eager/greedy' (here figuratively 'longing'). 'Andavan' is the imperfect plural of andare ('they were going' / 'kept turning'); 'pur' = 'still/even'; 'al cielo' = 'to the sky/ heaven' (a + il).
segments.purgatorio-8-g96: Another 'pur' ('even/still') + locative 'là' ('there'). 'Dove' introduces a relative clause 'where.' 'Le stelle son più tarde' uses 'son' (contracted sono) 'are' and 'più tarde' 'later/slower' — i.e., 'there where the stars are later' (rising later or moving more slowly in the celestial order).
segments.purgatorio-8-g97: 'Sì come' = 'just as' (introducing a simile). 'Rota' is a feminine noun 'wheel' used metaphorically; 'più presso' = 'closer' and 'a lo stelo' = 'to the stalk/shaft' (lo = the). The comparison says the eyes moved upward like a wheel closer to its stalk/axis (Dante's poetic image about relative motion).
segments.purgatorio-8-g98: E is the coordinating conjunction 'and', linking this speech to the previous line; '’l' is the contracted article + pronoun for 'il' (the) before a noun; 'duca' here means 'guide' (Dante uses it of Virgil) rather than a noble duke; 'mio' is a possessive agreeing in gender/number with 'duca' (my guide). The whole segment is an introductory tag that leads into direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-8-g99: Figliuol is a vocative address, an archaic spelling of 'figliol'/'figlio' used as 'son' or 'child' in direct speech; the comma marks the pause before the question.
segments.purgatorio-8-g100: Che functions as an interrogative 'what' here (not a relative 'that'); là sù is a two-word locative 'up there' (là = there, sù = up/above); guarde is an archaic form of the verb 'guardare' (3rd singular present), meaning 'looks at' or 'is looking at' — the subjunctive/archaic spelling does not change the straightforward interrogative sense.
segments.purgatorio-8-g101: Simple punctuation: a full stop ending the line/speech.
segments.purgatorio-8-g102: E 'io a lui:' is an elliptic reporting phrase: 'And I to him:' meaning 'and I (said) to him'; 'io' is the subject 'I', 'a' is the preposition 'to', and 'lui' is the unstressed indirect-object pronoun 'him'. The verb 'said' is omitted (a common poetic ellipsis) because the colon introduces direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-8-g103: A quelle tre facelle: 'A' here introduces the object of the speaker's attention ('to/at those three torches'); 'quelle' = 'those' (feminine plural) modifies 'facelle'. 'Facelle' is a poetic plural of 'facella' meaning bright lights/torches/stars. 'di che' = 'of which' (relative 'che' = 'which'); '’l polo di qua' = 'the pole on this side' (di qua = on this side); 'tutto quanto' is an emphatic expression meaning 'altogether/all that'; 'arde' is present 'burns'. The whole relative clause describes the torches as causing the pole/sky on this side to be all aflame.
segments.purgatorio-8-g104: Ond' (short for 'onde'/'ond') functions as 'whereupon' or 'thereupon', introducing the reply; 'elli a me' should read as 'he to me' (i.e. 'he said to me'), with 'a' marking the indirect object and 'me' = 'me'. (Note: the lemma/sense list supplied for 'elli' in the dataset seems mismatched; here it functions as the pronoun 'he'.)
segments.purgatorio-8-g105: Le quattro chiare stelle — 'Le' is the plural article 'the'; 'quattro' is 'four'; 'chiare' is an adjective 'bright/clear' agreeing with 'stelle' (stars). 'Che vedevi staman' is a relative clause: 'che' = 'which'/'that', 'vedevi' is the imperfect 'you were seeing' / 'you saw' (2nd singular), and 'staman' is an archaic/dialectal form of 'stamane' = 'this morning'.
segments.purgatorio-8-g106: son di là basse — 'son' is the contracted present of 'sono' (they are); 'di là' = 'over there' / 'down there'; 'basse' is the plural feminine adjective 'low' (i.e. low in the sky). Together: 'are low down over there.'
segments.purgatorio-8-g107: e queste son salite ov’ eran quelle — 'e' = 'and'; 'queste' = 'these' (the present lights); 'son salite' is a compound perfect with 'essere' ('sono salite') meaning 'have risen' (they rose up); 'ov’' = 'ove'/'ov' = 'where'; 'eran' = imperfect 'they were' (there); 'quelle' = 'those (former ones)'. So: 'and these have risen where those were.'
segments.purgatorio-8-g152: Ed is the archaic/poetic form of e meaning “and” and here simply introduces speech. Ellì is used as a speaker label (“he”) but the provided lemma/senses do not include the personal pronoun; in the line it functions as the subject ‘he’ (EGLI) followed by a colon to mark direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-8-g153: Or is an adverb meaning “now” (a common poetic use). Va is the imperative (tu) of andare, here telling the addressee to go: literal = “Now go.” The two words form a short independent command starting the speech.
segments.purgatorio-8-g154: This subordinate clause begins with che used causally/introducing the reason (“for/because/that”). Sol here is the poetic form (contextually ‘the sun’), but the supplied senses list does not include the astronomical sense (so -1). Non si ricorca is a negative + reflexive verb: si is the reflexive clitic and ricorca (ricorcare) here means ‘recur/return’ (so: “does not recur/come back”). Sette volte = “seven times” (volte = occasions/turns). Nel letto = “in the bed,” with letto used metaphorically/astronomically as the house/bed of a zodiac sign. The relative che introduces the following relative clause describing that bed and links to Montone as its subject.
segments.purgatorio-8-g155: This clause continues the relative started by che. Cotesta = archaic demonstrative “this” (used instead of questa). Cortese modifies oppinïone as an ironic or courtly qualification. Oppinïone = “opinion” or reputation. Ti = indirect/object clitic “to you.” Fia is an archaic future form meaning “will be” (future of essere in Dantean usage). Chiavata is a nominalized past participle from chiavare meaning literally a fastening or nailing (here metaphorical ‘will be driven/nailed’). In mezzo de la testa = “into the middle of the head.” Con maggior chiovi = “with larger nails” (maggior = comparative “greater/ larger”; chiovi = nails). Che functioning here is comparative “than” and d’altrui sermone = “than another’s words/discourse” (altrui = of others; sermone = speech/discourse). The whole phrase is a vivid metaphor: the ‘courtly opinion’ will be nailed into your head more forcibly than others’ sermons would do.
segments.purgatorio-8-g156: Se introduces a conditional clause “if.” Corso di giudicio = “course/flow of judgment” (corso here = course/run; di giudicio = of judgment). Non s’arresta is a negative reflexive/passive-like construction: s’ is the reflexive clitic attached to arresta (present indicative of arrestare) meaning “does not stop/cease.” Together: “if the course of judgment does not stop.”
segments.purgatorio-8-g122: Sentendo is a gerund (present participle) expressing circumstance or cause: “on hearing”/“when they heard.” Fender is the infinitive “to split/cleave”; here it completes the gerund (hearing the splitting). L’ aere is the direct object of fender, and a + le verdi ali indicates the instrument/means (“by/with the green wings”). Word order (gerund + verb phrase) is idiomatic and compressed in poetry.
segments.purgatorio-8-g123: Fuggì is the passato remoto (simple past) third person singular of fuggire: “he/it fled.” ’l (elided il) is the definite article before serpente, the subject noun that follows in inverted order typical of poetry.
segments.purgatorio-8-g124: E is the coordinating conjunction “and.” Li is an archaic plural article (“the”). Angeli is the plural subject. Dier here is an archaic past form meaning “gave”/“made” (as in dar volta = to make a turn); it does not mean “say” here, so no offered sense fits. Volta is a noun “a turn”/“a circuit,” object of the verb.
segments.purgatorio-8-g125: Suso is an adverb meaning “upwards”/“above.” A le poste = “to the posts/stations” (poste = positions or stations). Rivolando is a present participle/gerund (“turning back”/“returning by flight”), describing how they move; iguali is an adjective plural (“equal”/“alike”) agreeing with the implied angels and characterizing their flight.
segments.purgatorio-8-g126: L’ombra is the noun with elided article (“the shade/shadow” or here ‘the spirit’). Che introduces a relative clause. S’era is the reflexive clitic + imperfect of essere, used as auxiliary (si era raccolta) to form the pluperfect/passive meaning “had gathered.” Al giudice = “around/about the judge” (a + il). Raccolta is the past participle agreeing with ombra. Quando chiamò is a temporal clause “when he called.”
segments.purgatorio-8-g127: Per tutto quello assalto means “throughout that whole assault/onslaught.” Punto is an adverbial intensifier meaning “not for a moment/at no point.” Non fu is the simple past/passive “was not.” Da me marks the agent/source (“by me”/“from me”). Guardare is an infinitive here that takes sciolta (past participle, feminine) as complement: the passive construction literally says she “was not freed from being looked at by me,” i.e. she was never free of my gaze.
segments.purgatorio-8-g128: Se introduces a conditional (“if”). La lucerna che ti mena in alto is a noun phrase with a relative clause (che ti mena = “that leads you”); mena is present indicative of menare. Truovi is a present tense second-person form (poetic variant of trovi) = “you find”; here it is governed by the conditional clause. Nel tuo arbitrio = “in your judgment/discretion.” Tanta cera quant’ è mestiere = “so much wax as is needed” (quant’ = quanto/‘as much as’; è mestiere = “is necessary/needed”). Infino al sommo smalto = “even/up to the highest polish/gloss.”
segments.purgatorio-8-g129: Cominciò is the passato remoto (simple past) third person singular of cominciare: “she began.” Ella is the subject pronoun “she” (used for emphasis).
segments.purgatorio-8-g130: Se here again introduces a conditional (“if”). Novella vera means “true news” (novella = news/tidings; vera = true). Di Val di Magra o di parte vicina = “of the Val di Magra or of a neighboring district.” Sai is the present second-person singular “you know” — the protasis asks whether the addressee knows such news.
segments.purgatorio-8-g131: Dillo is an imperative formed from dire (di’- + lo): “tell it”/“say it.” A me = “to me.” The printed lemma for dillo was an unrelated verb; here the form is the familiar imperative, telling the addressee to report the news.
segments.purgatorio-8-g132: Che introduces a subordinate clause (“for/that”). Già means “already.” Grande là era = “great was there” (era = past tense of essere); the word order is inverted for poetic effect, so literally “that already great there was.”
segments.purgatorio-8-g133: Fui is the passato remoto first-person singular of essere: “I was.” Chiamato is the past participle “called/named,” so the phrase means “I was called (named) Currado Malaspina.” Proper names follow the participle in apposition.
segments.purgatorio-8-g134: Non son is the present tense (son = sono) with negation: “I am not.” L’antico = “the ancient/old one” or “the former (one)”; the phrase denies identity with some earlier namesake.
segments.purgatorio-8-g135: Ma = “but.” Di lui discesi uses the preposition di with lui to mean “from him,” and discesi is the first-person past (passato remoto) of discendere: here genealogical—“I descend from him”/“I am descended from him.”
segments.purgatorio-8-g136: A’ miei = a + i miei (to/for my own people/family). Portai is printed with an incorrect lemma but here is the passato remoto first-person of portare: “I brought/carried.” L’amor = “the love.” Che introduces a relative clause; qui raffina is present indicative “that here refines/purifies,” meaning the love that is being refined in this place.
segments.purgatorio-8-g74: This is an exhortative clause: 'vieni' is the 2nd-person singular form of venire used here as an imperative ('come'). 'a veder' is the preposition a + infinitive vedere marking purpose ('to see' / 'come to see'). 'che' introduces a content clause explaining what to see. 'Dio per grazia volse' is a short subordinate clause: 'per grazia' = 'by/through grace' and 'volse' is the passato remoto (3rd person singular) of volere, an archaic/poetic past tense meaning 'willed' or 'desired.'
segments.purgatorio-8-g75: 'Poi' is an adverb meaning 'then' or 'afterwards' that establishes the temporal sequence before the reported speech.
segments.purgatorio-8-g76: 'vòlto a me' uses volto (past participle of volgere) as a participial adjective/verb meaning 'turned (toward) me'; the preposition a introduces the indirect object 'me'. The colon that follows marks direct speech beginning.
segments.purgatorio-8-g77: This clause begins with the preposition 'Per' meaning 'because of' or 'on account of' followed by 'quel singular grado' ('that peculiar/remarkable rank/degree'). 'che' is a relative pronoun introducing the subordinate clause. 'tu dei' is an archaic/poetic second-person singular form meaning 'you give/assign' (modern Italian 'dai'); the following 'a colui' marks the indirect object 'to him.' The rest ('che sì nasconde lo suo primo perché') continues the relative clause: 'che' = 'who/that', 'sì' = 'so', 'nasconde' = present indicative 'hides', and 'lo suo primo perché' uses 'perché' as a noun meaning 'first why' or 'original reason.'
segments.purgatorio-8-g78: This short clause 'che non lì è guado' is a parenthetical relative: 'che' = 'which', 'non' negates, 'lì' = 'there', 'è' = present 'is', and 'guado' = 'ford' (i.e., a crossing) — so 'which there is not a ford' = 'for which there is no crossing there.'
segments.purgatorio-8-g79: 'quando sarai di là da le larghe onde' is a temporal clause introduced by 'quando' ('when'). 'sarai' is the future 2nd-person singular of essere ('you will be'), 'di là da' = 'on the other side of' or 'beyond', 'le larghe onde' = 'the broad waves' (where 'le' is the feminine plural article and 'larghe' agrees with 'onde').
segments.purgatorio-8-g80: 'dì' is the imperative or present indicative form of dire used here as an imperative meaning 'say/tell.' 'a Giovanna mia' = 'to my Giovanna' (indirect object). The clause 'che per me chiami' uses 'che' to introduce the content of the message: 'per me' = 'for me' or 'on my behalf' and 'chiami' is the 2nd-person singular present subjunctive or imperative form of chiamare in this indirect command context meaning 'that she call' or 'that you call (her to do so)' — in Dante this form expresses the requested action. 'là dove a li ’nnocenti si risponde' is a relative/adverbial clause: 'là dove' = 'there where', 'a li ’nnocenti' = 'to the innocents', and 'si risponde' is the impersonal or passive-like construction 'is answered'/'there answers' (literally 'one answers' or 'is answered').
segments.purgatorio-8-g81: 'Non credo che' is a standard construction requiring the subjunctive in the subordinate clause: 'Non credo' = 'I do not believe', 'che' introduces the subordinate clause, and 'la sua madre più m’ami' contains 'più' meaning 'any longer' and 'm’ami' which is 'mi' (me) + 'ami' (present subjunctive of amare) — hence 'that his mother loves me any longer' with 'ami' in subjunctive because of the main verb of doubt. Note: in Dantean/archaic forms the clitic may appear before the verb as 'm’ami.'
segments.purgatorio-8-g82: 'poscia che' = 'since/after' introduces a causal/time clause. 'trasmutò' is the passato remoto (3rd person singular) of trasmutare meaning 'changed' or 'exchanged.' 'le bianche bende' = 'the white bandages/kerchiefs' with 'le' definite article and 'bianche' agreeing in gender and number with 'bende.'
segments.purgatorio-8-g83: 'le quai convien che' continues the remark with a relative 'quai' (archaic for 'which') referring back to 'bende.' 'convien' (from convenire) is an impersonal/personal present form meaning 'it is necessary' or 'it behooves' — here it governs a following subordinate clause introduced by 'che.'
segments.purgatorio-8-g84: 'misera!' is a direct exclamation addressing someone as 'wretched' or 'poor wretch' — an interjection of contempt or pity aimed at the woman just described; it stands outside the strict syntactic clause as an interjection.
segments.purgatorio-8-g85: This segment is a punctuation mark (a comma) connecting the exclamation of the previous segment to the short following clause.
segments.purgatorio-8-g86: 'ancor' (archaic form of 'ancora') means 'still' or 'yet.' 'brami' is the 2nd-person singular present of bramare (an older verb meaning 'desire' or 'long for') and here addresses the woman with 'you' implied: 'you still desire/long.' The short clause is elliptical: the object is implied from the prior context.
segments.purgatorio-8-g108: Com’ is a contracted form of Come (the apostrophe marks the elision) meaning “as/while.” ei is an archaic third-person singular pronoun “he.” parlava is the imperfect indicative (3rd singular) of parlare, “was speaking,” so the whole phrase gives the temporal background: “As he was speaking.”
segments.purgatorio-8-g109: e = “and.” Sordello is the proper name (the subject). a sé = “to himself,” a reflexive prepositional phrase indicating Sordello drew someone toward himself. il is the clitic object pronoun (old/poetic order here; modern Italian would be lo) meaning “him.” trasse is passato remoto (3rd sg) of trarre, “drew.” dicendo is the present participle “saying,” linking into the direct speech that follows.
segments.purgatorio-8-g110: This is the reported speech introduced by dicendo. Vedi is the imperative (or present-tense exclamation) of vedere, here “look”/“see.” là ’l is a contracted sequence for là il (yonder — the), with the article adjacent to the demonstrative. nostro avversaro = “our adversary.” The clause is direct address: “Look there — our adversary.”
segments.purgatorio-8-g111: e = “and.” drizzò is passato remoto (3rd sg) of drizzare, here “raised/pointed.” il dito = “the finger.” perché introduces a purpose clause and here means “so that.” ’n is an archaic contraction (see note below) and là = “there/that way.” guardasse is the imperfect subjunctive (3rd sg) of guardare used in a purpose sense after perché: “so that he might look (that way).”
segments.purgatorio-8-g112: Da = “from.” quella parte = “that side/part.” onde is a relative adverb meaning “from which/where,” introducing the subordinate clause. non ha riparo = present tense “has no shelter/refuge” (ha = has). la picciola vallea = “the small/little valley” (vallea is a poetic spelling). The whole phrase means “from that side where the little valley has no shelter.”
segments.purgatorio-8-g113: era is the imperfect (3rd singular) of essere, narratively “there was” or “there came” (was present). una = the indefinite article “a.” biscia is a snake, specifically a grass-snake in older usage; word order (verb before noun) is normal in poetry.
segments.purgatorio-8-g114: forse = “perhaps.” qual is an indefinite/relative pronoun here meaning “such as” or “like the one that,” introducing the clause that follows. diede is passato remoto (3rd sg) of dare, “gave.” ad Eva = “to Eve.” il cibo amaro = “the bitter food.” The clause suggests the snake is perhaps the sort that gave Eve the bitter food (i.e., the serpent of Eden).
segments.purgatorio-8-g115: Tra ... e ... = “between/among.” l’erba = “the grass” (elided form l’). ’ fior is a poetic elision for il fior (the flower/flowers). venìa is the imperfect of venire, “was coming”/“came” (narrative imperfect). la mala striscia = “the bad/evil strip/creeping thing”; striscia here names a creeping creature (the serpent) described as a strip/creeping trail. The line locates the snake between grass and flowers.
segments.purgatorio-8-g116: volgendo is the present participle of volgere, “turning.” ad ora ad or is a set phrase meaning “now this way, now that way” (ora and or are variant words for “now” used to indicate alternation). la testa = “the head.” The participle attaches to the serpent (subject implied): it was turning its head from side to side.
segments.purgatorio-8-g117: e = “and.” ’l dosso = poetic contraction for il dosso, “the back/ridge” (i.e., the serpent’s back). leccando is the present participle of leccare, “licking.” come bestia che si liscia = “like a beast that smooths/grooms itself;” che si liscia is a relative clause with si liscia (present of lisciare) meaning it smooths or grooms its coat. The participial phrase describes the serpent’s simultaneous action of licking its back.
segments.purgatorio-8-g118: Io = “I.” non vidi is passato remoto (1st singular) of vedere, “I did not see.” This is a straightforward negative perfective statement about the narrator’s perception.
segments.purgatorio-8-g119: e = “and.” però here means “therefore/so.” dicer is an archaic infinitive form of dire (modern dire); the infinitive precedes the finite verb (a poetic inversion). non posso is present tense “I cannot.” Together the phrase means “and therefore I cannot say.”
segments.purgatorio-8-g120: come = “how”/“in what way,” introducing the subordinate clause. mosser is the passato remoto (3rd plural) of muovere (here given as mossere), “moved.” li astor celestïali = “the celestial goshawks/hawks” (li = the plural article, astor = hawks/goshawks, celestïali = heavenly/celestial). The clause means “how the heavenly hawks moved.”
segments.purgatorio-8-g121: ma = “but.” vidi bene = “I saw clearly” (vidi = passato remoto of vedere; bene = well/clearly). e l’uno e l’altro = “both the one and the other” (i.e., both of them). mosso is a past participle meaning “moved”; the structure means “I clearly saw both of them moved” (i.e., in motion). The phrasing and word order are poetic but express that he witnessed both in movement.
segments.purgatorio-8-g137: Interjection: a brief exclamation (vocative/affective) standing alone; punctuation and the quotation mark show it is spoken aloud. No grammar beyond being an emotional interjection.
segments.purgatorio-8-g138: Just a punctuation mark: the comma separates the narrator's inserted report from the surrounding speech. It marks a short pause before the reported words.
segments.purgatorio-8-g139: diss’ is a poetic contraction of the passato remoto 'dissi' (first person singular of dire), so the literal structure is 'dissi io' = 'I said'; the verb precedes the subject (verb–subject order) for poetic effect. 'lui' here functions as the indirect object 'to him' (not a nominative subject).
segments.purgatorio-8-g140: per = 'through' or 'in' (here 'in/through your lands'); li is the archaic plural definite article ('the') used with paesi; vostri paesi = 'your regions/lands'. The negative phrase già mai non fui uses già (an adverb with emphatic/temporal force), mai with non to produce 'never', and fui is the first-person passato remoto of essere 'I was' (so: 'I have never been'). Word order is slightly archaic/poetic (adverbs before the auxiliary/past form).
segments.purgatorio-8-g141: ma = 'but'. dove = 'where' introducing the question. si dimora uses the impersonal/reflexive si with the verb dimorare ('one dwells' or 'does one dwell'), forming a general question about residence. per tutta Europa = 'throughout all Europe' (per used as 'throughout'). ch’ = contraction of che ('that') introducing a relative/subordinate clause; ei is an archaic plural pronoun 'they' (not singular 'he'); sien is an archaic subjunctive form (from essere) corresponding to modern 'siano' and expresses a non-assertive/possible quality; palesi is the plural adjective 'palese' meaning 'manifest/known/evident'. Altogether the clause asks where one could dwell throughout Europe such that 'they' would not be evident/known.
segments.purgatorio-8-g142: Noun phrase with relative clause: La fama 'the reputation/fame' is the head noun; che introduces the relative clause whose verb onora (present indicative 3rd sing.) takes la vostra casa as its object — literally 'the fame that honors your house' (i.e. the fame which brings honor to your family/lineage).
segments.purgatorio-8-g143: Two coordinated clauses with the present verb grida repeated for emphasis: grida (3rd sing. present) is used poetically to report that the fame is loudly proclaimed by both i segnori 'the lords' and la contrada 'the district/people'. The verb is singular because the (singular) fame is the implied agent doing the 'crying out'.
segments.purgatorio-8-g144: Result/so-consequence clause: sì che = 'so that' (introducing consequence). ne sa 'knows of it' uses the pronominal ne = 'of it/about it'. chi non vi fu ancora is a relative/indefinite 'he who has not yet been there' where fu is passato remoto of essere ('was/has been') and vi is locative 'there'; ancora = 'yet'.
segments.purgatorio-8-g145: Simple main clause: e io vi giuro = 'and I swear to you' with the present indicative giuro 'I swear' and dative/locative vi 'to you' (addressing the addressee).
segments.purgatorio-8-g146: Conditional clause with archaic contraction: s’io is contracted from se io 'if I', and di sopra 'up above/there above' is a locative; vada is the present subjunctive of andare used here in a conditional sense ('if I go up')—older Italian often uses the subjunctive where modern English uses a simple future/conditional.
segments.purgatorio-8-g147: Resulting clause introduced by che: vostra gente onrata 'your honored people' is the subject; non si sfregia is a negative reflexive verb (si = themselves) in present indicative, meaning 'will not dishonor/disgrace themselves' in the sense of a promise of future conduct; del pregio de la borsa e de la spada = 'concerning the value/esteem of purse (money) and sword (arms)' where del/de la are partitives/'of the'.
segments.purgatorio-8-g148: Uso e natura is the compound subject ('uso' = custom/habit, 'natura' = nature). sì is an adverb meaning 'so' or 'thus'. la is the feminine third-person singular direct-object clitic placed before the verb. privilegia is present indicative, 3rd-person singular; in medieval/poetic usage a compound subject can take a singular verb or the verb may agree by attraction with the nearer idea, so the singular privilegia governs the clitic la.
segments.purgatorio-8-g149: che is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing the following explanatory/casual subordinate clause; it links the main statement to the reason or consequence which follows.
segments.purgatorio-8-g150: perché here means 'because' (giving the cause). il capo reo is the noun phrase 'the guilty head' with the adjective reo postposed in the typical Italian word order. il mondo is the direct object 'the world', and torca is the 3rd-person singular present (in Dante -a form) of torcere; the verb appears at the end (poetic inversion) and, with perché, expresses the causal relation 'because the guilty head turns/distorts the world'.
segments.purgatorio-8-g151: sola is feminine singular 'alone/only', referring back to the feminine object 'la' and functioning as the subject of the verbs. va dritta is the fixed phrase 'goes straight/keeps the right course' (va = 3rd-person sing. present indicative; dritta = 'straight' as predicate adjective). e coordinates the next verb; ’l is the elided article 'il' before the noun phrase mal cammin ('bad/evil path' — cammin = poetic form of cammino), and dispregia is 3rd-person singular present indicative 'despises/scorns', with the same feminine subject.

sentences.purgatorio-8-s18: I said to him, "This morning I came through those doleful places, and I am in my first life, although the other I shall gain as I go on."
sentences.purgatorio-8-s19: And when my answer was heard, Sordello and he drew back like people suddenly bewildered.
sentences.purgatorio-8-s20: One turned to Virgil and the other to a man who was sitting there, shouting: "Up, Currado!"
sentences.purgatorio-8-s9: “Both come from the womb of Mary,” Sordello said, “to guard the valley against the serpent that will come more and more.”
sentences.purgatorio-8-s10: So I, who did not know by which path, turned around and drew close, all frozen, to the trusted shoulders.
sentences.purgatorio-8-s11: And Sordello too: “Now let us go down among the great shades, and we will speak to them; they will be very pleased to see you.”
sentences.purgatorio-8-s12: I think I descended only three steps, and I was below, and I saw one who was gazing straight at me, as if he wished to recognize me.
sentences.purgatorio-8-s5: I saw that gentle host silently afterward turn their gaze back upon them, as if waiting, pale and humble; and I saw two angels issue from on high and descend, bearing two heated swords, truncated and deprived of their points.
sentences.purgatorio-8-s6: They were green like little leaves just born, in a garment, which, struck by green feathers, drew them along behind and fanned them.
sentences.purgatorio-8-s7: One of them came a little above us to stand, and the other descended to the opposite bank, so that the people were held in the middle.
sentences.purgatorio-8-s8: I could well discern the fair blond head among them; but my eye was bewildered in the face, like a power that is confounded by excess.
sentences.purgatorio-8-s13: It was already the time when the air was growing dark, but not so (dark) that, between his eyes and mine, it would not reveal what it had earlier kept closed.
sentences.purgatorio-8-s14: He turned toward me, and I toward him: 'Judge Nino, gentle one, how pleased I was when I saw that you were not among the guilty!'
sentences.purgatorio-8-s15: No graceful greeting between us remained unspoken; then he asked: "How long is it since you came to the foot of the mountain by the far-off waters?"
sentences.purgatorio-8-s1: It was already the hour that turns longing toward those who sail and softens the heart on the day they have said farewell to their dear friends; and which pricks the new pilgrim of love, if he hears a bell ringing from afar that makes the day seem to weep as it dies; when I began to make listening vain and to gaze at one of the souls risen up, who was asking to be heard with her hand.
sentences.purgatorio-8-s2: She came and lifted both palms, fixing her eyes toward the East, as if to say to God: 'Comfort me with nothing else.'
sentences.purgatorio-8-s3: 'Te lucis ante' issued from her mouth so devoutly and with such sweet notes that it made me lose my mind; and the others then, gently and devoutly, followed her through the whole hymn, their eyes fixed upon the heavenly wheels.
sentences.purgatorio-8-s4: Reader, sharpen your eyes well here to the truth, for the veil is now so thin that surely passing through it inward is easy.
sentences.purgatorio-8-s25: From her one can readily understand how long the flame of love endures in a woman, if the eye or the touch does not often kindle it.
sentences.purgatorio-8-s26: Not even the viper that Melanesi encamps will give her so fine a burial.
sentences.purgatorio-8-s27: Thus he was saying, his expression stamped by that upright zeal which moderately blazes in the heart.
sentences.purgatorio-8-s28: My greedy eyes still kept turning to the sky, even there where the stars are later, just like a wheel nearer to the stalk.
sentences.purgatorio-8-s29: And my guide: 'Son, what are you looking at up there?'
sentences.purgatorio-8-s31: And I to him: 'To those three torches of which the pole on this side burns wholly.'
sentences.purgatorio-8-s32: Whereupon he to me: 'The four bright stars that you saw this morning are down there low, and these have risen where those were.'
sentences.purgatorio-8-s45: And he said: “Now go; for the sun does not recur seven times in the bed that the Ram, with all four feet, covers and plants, and that courtly opinion will be driven into the middle of your head with larger nails than another’s words, if the course of judgment does not stop.”
sentences.purgatorio-8-s37: Hearing the air split by their green wings, the serpent fled, and the angels turned, flying back to the stations above, equal in flight.
sentences.purgatorio-8-s38: The shade that had gathered about the judge when he called, throughout that whole onset was not at any point freed from my gaze.
sentences.purgatorio-8-s39: “If you, in your discretion, find that the lamp which leads you upward has so much wax as is needed even to the highest sheen,” she began, “if you know any true news of the Val di Magra or of a neighboring district, tell me, for great was already there.”
sentences.purgatorio-8-s40: I was called Currado Malaspina; I am not the ancient one, but I descended from him; to my people I brought the love that is refined here.”
sentences.purgatorio-8-s21: Come and see what God willed by grace.
sentences.purgatorio-8-s22: Then, turning to me, he said: 'Because of that singular degree you assign to him who so hides his first “why” — which is no ford there — when you are beyond the broad waves, tell my Giovanna to call for me where the innocent are answered.'
sentences.purgatorio-8-s23: I do not believe that his mother loves me any longer, now that she has changed the white bandages, which are proper—wretch!
sentences.purgatorio-8-s33: As he was speaking, Sordello drew him to himself, saying, "Look there — our adversary"; and he raised his finger so that he might look that way.
sentences.purgatorio-8-s34: From that side where the little valley has no shelter, there came a snake, perhaps like the one that gave Eve the bitter food.
sentences.purgatorio-8-s35: Between the grass and the flowers came the wicked serpent, turning its head now one way now another, and licking its back like a beast that smooths itself.
sentences.purgatorio-8-s36: I did not see (it), and therefore I cannot say how the celestial hawks moved; but I clearly saw both of them move.
sentences.purgatorio-8-s42: I said to him, "Through your lands I have never been; but where does one dwell throughout all Europe so that they may not be known?"
sentences.purgatorio-8-s43: The fame that honors your house cries out among the lords and cries out in the district, so that even someone who has not yet been there knows of it; and I swear to you, if I go up above, that your honored people will not disgrace themselves concerning the worth of purse and sword.
sentences.purgatorio-8-s44: Custom and nature so favor her that, because the guilty head twists the world, she alone goes straight and scorns the wicked path.
